You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

  <requested_at>2026-07-12</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING START
=======================

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
The user is reviewing the bridge codebase for optimization opportunities. The grep shows extensive use of synchronous file operations (readFileSync, writeFileSync, etc.) across many files in the src/ directory. This is a classic performance issue - synchronous file I/O blocks the event loop.

This appears to be a discovery phase where they're identifying potential optimization points. I should record this as a discovery observation about the finding that there are many synchronous file operations that could be blocking.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Bridge codebase uses synchronous file I/O extensively</title>
  <subtitle>200+ instances of blocking file operations found across 40+ source files</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Synchronous file operations (readFileSync, writeFileSync, existsSync, readdirSync, appendFileSync, statSync) appear 200+ times across src/ directory</fact>
    <fact>High-traffic modules use sync I/O: run-prompt.ts (1837 lines), index.ts (1692 lines), sessionManager.ts (1190 lines)</fact>
    <fact>Memory-intensive modules heavily affected: facts-store.ts (25+ sync calls), memory.ts, page-index.ts, ingest-ripple.ts</fact>
    <fact>Recent commits (cdf1ff3..136f0c0) focused on streaming fixes and MCP memory, not file I/O optimization</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Analysis of the telegram-kiro-bridge codebase revealed extensive use of synchronous file I/O operations blocking the Node.js event loop. A grep search found 200+ instances of readFileSync, writeFileSync, existsSync, readdirSync, appendFileSync, and statSync across 40+ source files. The largest modules (run-prompt.ts at 1837 lines, index.ts at 1692 lines, sessionManager.ts at 1190 lines) all use synchronous file operations. Memory management modules like facts-store.ts, memory.ts, page-index.ts, and ingest-ripple.ts are particularly affected with 25+ sync file calls each. This pattern creates potential event loop blocking during file-heavy operations like loading wiki indexes, reading activity logs, or saving session state. Recent work (5 commits since cdf1ff3) addressed streaming animations, MCP memory dependencies, and UI rendering but did not tackle file I/O performance.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Bridge uses multiple interval-based polling timers</title>
  <subtitle>Four setInterval timers found: relay polling (3s), heartbeat, session sweeper (60s), podcast</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>relay.ts maintains two timers: pollTimer polling every 3000ms and heartbeatTimer for connection keepalive</fact>
    <fact>sessionManager.ts runs sweeper every 60_000ms (1 minute) to clean idle sessions</fact>
    <fact>podcast.ts uses setInterval timer for periodic updates</fact>
    <fact>Multiple modules use 1000-1100ms setTimeout delays for rate-limiting API calls</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Analysis identified four active setInterval timers in the bridge codebase. The relay module maintains two timers: pollTimer checking for incoming messages every 3 seconds and heartbeatTimer for connection keepalive. The sessionManager runs a sweeper every 60 seconds to garbage-collect idle sessions. The podcast module uses a timer for periodic content updates. Several modules (bot-setup.ts, run-prompt.ts, local-llm.ts) use 1000-1100ms setTimeout delays for rate-limiting sequential operations. These polling patterns could potentially be replaced with event-driven or webhook-based approaches to reduce idle CPU wake-ups, though the current intervals are reasonable for their use cases.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>

\\\"./telegram-rich-renderer.js\\\";\\nimport { coalesceAsyncRunner } from \\\"./async-coalesce.js\\\";\\nimport { quotaHint } from \\\"./provider/quota-error.js\\\";\\n\\n// ─── Types auto-selection ──────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\nconst EMOTION_REPRS: Record<string, string> = {\\n  hello: \\\"打招呼 你好 嗨 早安 晚安 greeting\\\",\\n  happy: \\\"開心 太好了 成功 完成了 恭喜 great\\\",\\n  thanks: \\\"謝謝 感謝 thank you 不客氣\\\",\\n  sorry: \\\"抱歉 對不起 不好意思 sorry\\\",\\n  agree: \\\"同意 沒問題 好的 了解 ok sure\\\",\\n  good: \\\"做得好 讚 很棒 nice well done\\\",\\n  clap: \\\"恭喜 太厲害了 完美 bravo\\\",\\n  love: \\\"喜歡 愛 太棒了 超讚\\\",\\n  sad: \\\"可惜 遺憾 不幸 unfortunately\\\",\\n  shock: \\\"驚訝 天啊 沒想到 wow unexpected\\\",\\n  ok: \\\"收到 了解 知道了 acknowledged\\\",\\n};\\nconst STICKER_AUTO_THRESHOLD = 0.65;\\nconst STICKER_AUTO_MAX_REPLY_LEN = 150;\\nlet emotionVectors: Map<string, Float32Array> | null = null;\\n\\nexport async function autoSelectSticker(reply: string): Promise<string | null> {\\n  if (reply.length > STICKER_AUTO_MAX_REPLY_LEN || reply.length < 5) return null;\\n  let vectors = emotionVectors;\\n  if (!vectors) {\\n    vectors = new Map();\\n    let complete = true;\\n    for (const [emotion, repr] of Object.entries(EMOTION_REPRS)) {\\n      const vec = await embedText(repr);\\n      if (vec) vectors.set(emotion, vec);\\n      else complete = false;\\n    }\\n    // 只有全部成功才寫入快取：embed model 暖機中部分失敗若被永久快取，\\n    // 缺席的情緒向量到重啟前都不會補算（快取成空 Map 更是全滅）。\\n    if (complete) emotionVectors = vectors;\\n  }\\n  const queryVec = await embedText(reply);\\n  if (!queryVec) return null;\\n  let best = \\\"\\\";\\n  let bestScore = 0;\\n  for (const [emotion, vec] of vectors) {\\n    const score = cosineSim(queryVec, vec);\\n    if (score > bestScore) { bestScore = score; best = emotion; }\\n  }\\n  return bestScore >= STICKER_AUTO_THRESHOLD ? best : null;\\n}\\n\\n// ─── Continuation timer ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\nconst GOAL_CONTINUATION_DELAY_MS = 500;\\nconst pendingContinuations = new Map<number, NodeJS.Timeout>();\\n\\nexport function cancelPendingContinuation(chatId: number): void {\\n  const h = pendingContinuations.get(chatId);\\n  if (h) {\\n    clearTimeout(h);\\n    pendingContinuations.delete(chatId);\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n// ─── Deps interface — mutable state that lives in index.ts ───────────────────\\n\\nexport interface RunPromptDeps {\\n  bot: Bot;\\n  sessions: SessionManager;\\n  rateLimitedUntil: () => number;\\n  onRateLimitRecovery: (fn: () => void | Promise<void>) => () => void;\\n  queuePendingMessage: (chatId: number, text: string) => void;\\n  handleRestart: (ctx: Context, opts?: { reason?: string }) => Promise<void>;\\n  STREAM_EDIT_INTERVAL_MS: number;\\n  MEMORY_DIR: string;\\n  scheduler: Scheduler;\\n  scheduleStore: ScheduleStore;\\n}\\n\\nlet _deps: RunPromptDeps | null = null;\\n\\nexport function initRunPrompt(deps: RunPromptDeps): void {\\n  _deps = deps;\\n}\\n\\nfunction deps(): RunPromptDeps {\\n  if (!_deps) throw new Error(\\\"runPrompt not initialized — call initRunPrompt first\\\");\\n  return _deps;\\n}\\n\\n// ─── Auto-wikilink post-processing ───────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\n/** After a wiki page is written, scan it and inject [[wikilinks]] for known entities. */\\nfunction applyAutoWikilink(slug: string): void {\\n  const { MEMORY_DIR } = deps();\\n  for (const sub of [\\\"queries\\\", \\\"concepts\\\", \\\"references\\\", \\\"lessons\\\", \\\"tasks\\\"]) {\\n    const filePath = path.join(MEMORY_DIR, \\\"wiki\\\", sub, `${slug}.md`);\\n    if (existsSync(filePath)) {\\n      try {\\n        const content = readFileSync(filePath, \\\"utf8\\\");\\n        const linked = autoWikilink(content);\\n        if (linked !== content) {\\n          writeFileSync(filePath, linked, \\\"utf8\\\");\\n          console.log(`[auto-wikilink] injected links into wiki/${sub}/${slug}.md`);\\n        }\\n      } catch (err) {\\n        console.error(`[auto-wikilink] failed for ${slug}:`, err);\\n      }\\n      return;\\n    }\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n// ─── Orphan turn flusher ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n//\\n// Delivers agent output that arrived with NO active prompt turn — e.g. a\\n// Claude Code harness background sub-agent completes and re-invokes the agent\\n// via task-notification, producing an agent_message_chunk while runPrompt's\\n// request→response flush path isn't listening (see SPEC-orphan-turn-flusher).\\n//\\n// SessionManager buffers + debounces those chunks and calls this on quiescence\\n// (wired via sessions.onOrphanTurn in index.ts). This reuses the SAME reply\\n// transform (transformReplyTokens) + render/split helpers as the normal turn —\\n// there is deliberately NO second transform. Tokens that only make sense inside\\n// a live turn (GOAL_DONE / RESTART / CONTINUE / WIKI_QUERY / SKILL_PROPOSE /\\n// SPECIALIST_PROXY / relay*) are intentionally ignored here; user-facing tokens\\n// (text / SEND_FILE / ASK / SCHEDULE / STICKER) are delivered.\\nexport async function flushOrphanTurn(session: ChatSession, rawText: string): Promise<void> {\\n  const { bot, rateLimitedUntil, queuePendingMessage, scheduler, scheduleStore } = deps();\\n  const chatId = session.chatId;\\n\\n  // Dedup: skip content already delivered (e.g. same summary that also came\\n  // through a normal turn, or a duplicate orphan flush). Shares the 5-min\\n  // window used by specialist/parallel result injection.\\n  if (isDuplicateResult(chatId, rawText)) {\\n    console.log(`[orphan ${chatId}] skipped (dedup hit)`);\\n    return;\\n  }\\n\\n  const ab7 = transformReplyTokens(rawText, {\\n    parseDuration,\\n    parseAbsoluteTime,\\n    parseRecurrence,\\n    computeNextRunAt,\\n  });\\n\\n  // SCHEDULE tokens — register the same way the normal path does.\\n  const scheduleReceipts: string[] = [];\\n  for (const se of ab7.scheduleTokens) {\\n    try {\\n      const entry = await scheduleStore.add({\\n        chatId,\\n        userId: session.userId ?? chatId,\\n        runAt: se.runAt,\\n        prompt: se.prompt,\\n        recurrence: se.recurrence,\\n      });\\n      scheduler.arm(entry);\\n      const delta = formatRelative(se.runAt - Date.now());\\n      const recurTag = se.recurrence ? ` [${formatRecurrence(se.recurrence)}]` : \\\"\\\";\\n      scheduleReceipts.push(\\n        `⏰ 已排程 ${entry.id}${recurTag}，${delta} 後觸發（${new Date(se.runAt).toLocaleString()}）`,\\n      );\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.error(`[orphan ${chatId}] schedule token register failed:`, err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // Surface (but don't act on) tokens that require live-turn context.\\n  const ignored: string[] = [];\\n  if (ab7.goalDone) ignored.push(\\\"GOAL_DONE\\\");\\n  if (ab7.restartToken) ignored.push(\\\"RESTART\\\");\\n  if (ab7.continueToken) ignored.push(\\\"CONTINUE\\\");\\n  if (ab7.wikiQuery) ignored.push(\\\"WIKI_QUERY\\\");\\n  if (ab7.skillPropose) ignored.push(\\\"SKILL_PROPOSE\\\");\\n  if (ab7.specialistProxy) ignored.push(\\\"SPECIALIST_PROXY\\\");\\n  if (ab7.moaPlan) ignored.push(\\\"MOA_PLAN\\\");\\n  if (ignored.length) {\\n    console.log(`[orphan ${chatId}] ignoring turn-context token(s): ${ignored.join(\\\", \\\")}`);\\n  }\\n\\n  const body = scheduleReceipts.length\\n    ? (ab7.text ? `${ab7.text}\\\\n\\\\n${scheduleReceipts.join(\\\"\\\\n\\\")}` : scheduleReceipts.join(\\\"\\\\n\\\"))\\n    : ab7.text;\\n  const askKeyboard = buildAskKeyboard(ab7.askTokens);\\n\\n  // Text delivery (chunked). Rate-limit aware: queue for replay if throttled.\\n  if (body.trim().length > 0) {\\n    const chunks = splitForTelegram(body);\\n    for (let i = 0; i < chunks.length; i++) {\\n      const isLast = i === chunks.length - 1;\\n      const pageTag = chunks.length > 1 ? `(${i + 1}/${chunks.length})\\\\n` : \\\"\\\";\\n      const text = pageTag + chunks[i];\\n      const extra = isLast && askKeyboard ? { reply_markup: askKeyboard } : undefined;\\n      if (rateLimitedUntil() > Date.now()) {\\n        queuePendingMessage(chatId, text);\\n        continue;\\n      }\\n      try {\\n        const msg = await bot.api.sendMessage(chatId, text, extra);\\n        if (isLast && askKeyboard && msg?.message_id !== undefined) {\\n          askRegistry.set(msg.message_id, ab7.askTokens);\\n        }\\n      } catch (err) {\\n        console.error(`[orphan ${chatId}] chunk send failed, queueing for replay:`, err);\\n        queuePendingMessage(chatId, text);\\n      }\\n      if (!isLast) await new Promise<void>((r) => setTimeout(r, 1100));\\n    }\\n  } else if (askKeyboard) {\\n    // ASK-only orphan turn (rare) — carry the keyboard on a minimal message.\\n    try {\\n      const msg = await bot.api.sendMessage(chatId, \\\"（背景任務完成）\\\", { reply_markup: askKeyboard });\\n      if (msg?.message_id !== undefined) askRegistry.set(msg.message_id, ab7.askTokens);\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.error(`[orphan ${chatId}] ask-only send failed:`, err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // SEND_FILE — sendFilesByPaths needs a Context-like; build a minimal shim.\\n  if (ab7.sendFiles.length > 0) {\\n    const fakeCtx = {\\n      chat: { id: chatId, type: \\\"private\\\" as const },\\n      replyWithPhoto: (f: unknown, extra?: unknown) => bot.api.sendPhoto(chatId, f as any, extra as any),\\n      replyWithDocument: (f: unknown, extra?: unknown) => bot.api.sendDocument(chatId, f as any, extra as any),\\n    } as unknown as Context;\\n    try {\\n      await sendFilesByPaths(fakeCtx, ab7.sendFiles);\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.error(`[orphan ${chatId}] sendFilesByPaths failed:`, err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // Stickers\\n  for (const emotion of ab7.stickerEmotions) {\\n    const fileId = STICKER_MAP[emotion];\\n    if (!fileId) continue;\\n    try {\\n      await bot.api.sendSticker(chatId, fileId);\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.warn(`[orphan ${chatId}] sticker ${emotion} failed — ${(err as Error).message}`);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  console.log(\\n    `[orphan ${chatId}] delivered (textChars=${body.length}, files=${ab7.sendFiles.length}, ` +\\n      `asks=${ab7.askTokens.length}, schedules=${scheduleReceipts.length}, stickers=${ab7.stickerEmotions.length})`,\\n  );\\n}\\n\\n// ─── runPrompt ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\nexport async function runPrompt(\\n  ctx: Context,\\n  session: ChatSession,\\n  text: string,\\n  mode: RunPromptMode = { kind: \\\"user\\\" },\\n) {\\n  const { bot, sessions, rateLimitedUntil, onRateLimitRecovery, queuePendingMessage,\\n    handleRestart, STREAM_EDIT_INTERVAL_MS, MEMORY_DIR, scheduler, scheduleStore } = deps();\\n\\n  const continuationContext: ContinuationContext | undefined =\\n    mode.kind === \\\"continuation\\\" ? mode.context : undefined;\\n  const isMeta = mode.kind === \\\"meta\\\";\\n  const isSilentMeta = mode.kind === \\\"meta\\\" && mode.silent === true;\\n  const relayReplyTo = mode.kind === \\\"relay\\\" ? mode.replyTo : undefined;\\n  const isRelay = mode.kind === \\\"relay\\\";\\n  // Optional header prepended to the visible reply (and shown in the \\\"Thinking…\\\"\\n  // placeholder). Used e.g. by ASK button clicks to record \\\"我選擇了：X\\\" in the\\n  // reply itself, so the original button message isn't rewritten and no extra\\n  // message is sent. Render-only — NOT added to agent history/memory.\\n  const replyHeader = mode.kind === \\\"user\\\" ? mode.replyHeader : undefined;\\n  const chatId = ctx.chat!.id;\\n\\n  // Guard against concurrent prompts on the same ACP session.\\n  if (session.inflight) {\\n    if (relayReplyTo) {\\n      sendRelay(relayReplyTo, \\\"小X 目前上一輪還在處理中，稍後再回覆。\\\").catch((err) =>\\n        console.error(`[chat ${chatId}] relay busy notice to ${relayReplyTo} failed:`, err),\\n      );\\n      notifyRelayOwner(\\n        bot,\\n        config.allowedUserIds[0] ?? 0,\\n        `⏳ 「${relayReplyTo}」又送來 relay 訊息，但上一輪還在處理中，已擋掉這次重複注入。`,\\n        `busy notice for ${relayReplyTo}`,\\n      );\\n      return;\\n    }\\n    if (isMeta) {\\n      console.log(`[chat ${chatId}] meta prompt queued (session inflight)`);\\n      session._pendingMetaQueue.push({ ctx: ctx as unknown as any, prompt: text, opts: mode as any });\\n      return;\\n    }\\n    await ctx.reply(\\n      \\\"⏳ 上一個回合還在進行中。請等它結束，或先用 /cancel 取消。\\\",\\n    );\\n    return;\\n  }\\n  session.inflight = true;\\n  sessions.resetTurn(session);\\n  // Turn 所有權 token：cancel() 樂觀釋放 session 後新 turn 可能接管共享欄位。\\n  // prompt() resolve 後與 finally 內都要驗這個 token，舊 turn 不得做收尾、\\n  // 也不得覆寫已屬於新 turn 的 onUpdate/inflight。\\n  session._turnSeq = (session._turnSeq ?? 0) + 1;\\n  const turnToken = session._turnSeq;\\n  const turnSuperseded = () content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Fixed memory leak in atomicJson updateJson pending write tracking</title>
  <subtitle>Added compare-and-delete cleanup to prevent writePendingByPath map growing unbounded</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>atomicJson.ts updateJson now calls finally() to remove settled promises from writePendingByPath map</fact>
    <fact>Cleanup uses compare-and-delete pattern: only removes entry if it matches the current promise to handle concurrent updates</fact>
    <fact>Memory leak existed because resolved/rejected promises stayed in the map indefinitely</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Fixed a memory leak in atomicJson.ts where the writePendingByPath map grew unbounded. The updateJson function previously added promises to the map but never removed them after settlement, causing the map to accumulate all historical write operations. The fix wraps the promise in chained.finally() that compares and deletes the entry only if it still matches the current promise, preventing concurrent updates from deleting each other's entries. This mirrors the same compare-and-delete pattern already used in atomicWriteJson. The leak would have grown slowly over long-running sessions performing many JSON updates.</narrative>

    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>

</observation>

<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Fixed MCP memory server crash by removing config.js dependency from ingest-ripple</title>
  <subtitle>Replaced config.memoryDir with resolveMemoryDir to avoid TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN requirement</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>ingest-ripple.ts imported config.js which requires TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN at module load time</fact>
    <fact>MCP memory subprocess intentionally sets TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN empty to prevent Telegram API 409 conflicts</fact>
    <fact>Module now uses facts-store.resolveMemoryDir() which has same env logic without requiring token</fact>
    <fact>All five path constructions (WIKI_INDEX_PATH, RIPPLE_LOG_PATH, pagePath, shardDir, .user-id) migrated to resolveMemoryDir()</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Fixed a startup crash in the MCP memory server subprocess caused by ingest-ripple.ts importing config.js. The config module validates TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN at load time, but the MCP subprocess intentionally clears this token to prevent 409 conflict errors when both bridge and MCP try to use the Telegram API. This created a catch-22: ingest-ripple needed config.memoryDir but couldn't import config without crashing. The fix removes the config.js import entirely and replaces all config.memoryDir references with calls to facts-store.resolveMemoryDir(), which implements the same MEMORY_DIR environment variable resolution and fallback logic without requiring the bot token. Five path construction sites were updated to use the new function.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
  </concepts>

    <file>src/ingest-ripple.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/ingest-ripple.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Added CONTINUE token to observerTransformer opener whitelist</title>
  <subtitle>Fixed mid-write token flashing for CONTINUE, matching existing RESTART bareword support</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>observerTransformer TOKEN_OPENER_NAMES list controls which tokens get hidden during streaming</fact>
    <fact>RESTART bareword already supported in regex as special case, CONTINUE was missing</fact>
    <fact>Regex pattern changed from <<RESTART(?::|>?$) to <<(?:RESTART|CONTINUE)(?::|>?$) for consistency</fact>
    <fact>Comment updated to note both RESTART and CONTINUE as bareword exceptions</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Fixed the observerTransformer to properly hide the CONTINUE token during streaming output. The module maintains a whitelist (TOKEN_OPENER_NAMES) of recognized tokens that should be hidden while being typed to prevent ugly mid-write flashing to users. RESTART already had special bareword support in the regex (no colon required), but CONTINUE was missing. This caused CONTINUE tokens to briefly flash visible during streaming before completion. The fix adds CONTINUE alongside RESTART in the regex pattern, treating both as bareword exceptions. The comment was updated to document both tokens as having special bareword handling, preventing future omissions when new tokens are added.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>

  </files_read>

  </files_modified>
</observation>

// 5 分鐘\\n\\ninterface DedupEntry {\\n  hash: string;\\n  timestamp: number;\\n}\\n\\n// chatId → 最近的 dedup entries\\nconst _cache = new Map<number, DedupEntry[]>();\\n\\n/**\\n * 計算文字的去重 hash（取前 2000 字做 SHA-256 前 16 hex）。\\n * 用前 2000 字是因為 specialist summary 最長就 2000。\\n */\\nfunction computeHash(text: string): string {\\n  const input = text.slice(0, 2000);\\n  return createHash(\\\"sha256\\\").update(input, \\\"utf8\\\").digest(\\\"hex\\\").slice(0, 16);\\n}\\n\\n/**\\n * 檢查是否重複。若不重複則登記並回傳 false；若重複回傳 true。\\n *\\n * @param chatId — Telegram chat ID\\n * @param summary — specialist result summary 文字\\n * @returns true = 重複（應跳過注入）, false = 新的（可以注入）\\n */\\nexport function isDuplicateResult(chatId: number, summary: string): boolean {\\n  const now = Date.now();\\n  const hash = computeHash(summary);\\n\\n  // 取出或初始化 cache\\n  let entries = _cache.get(chatId);\\n  if (!entries) {\\n    entries = [];\\n    _cache.set(chatId, entries);\\n  }\\n\\n  // 清理過期 entries\\n  const cutoff = now - DEDUP_WINDOW_MS;\\n  const fresh = entries.filter((e) => e.timestamp > cutoff);\\n\\n  // 檢查是否有相同 hash\\n  const dup = fresh.some((e) => e.hash === hash);\\n  if (dup) {\\n    _cache.set(chatId, fresh);\\n    return true; // 重複\\n  }\\n\\n  // 登記新 entry\\n  fresh.push({ hash, timestamp: now });\\n  _cache.set(chatId, fresh);\\n  return false; // 不重複\\n}\\n\\n/**\\n * 清除指定 chatId 的 dedup cache（例如 /reset 時）。\\n */\\nexport function clearDedupCache(chatId?: number): void {\\n  if (chatId !== undefined) {\\n    _cache.delete(chatId);\\n  } else {\\n    _cache.clear();\\n  }\\n}\\n\",\"numLines\":79,\"startLine\":1,\"totalLines\":79}}"</outcome>

content.thinking:
This is a substantial diff showing the implementation of a dual-channel streaming architecture for Telegram draft messages. Let me understand what was changed:

1. **run-prompt.ts changes:**
   - Added a separate "status message" channel for volatile content (tool progress, thinking preview)
   - The status message is sent once with `disable_notification: true` and then edited in place
   - It gets deleted when the turn finalizes
   - This separates volatile content from the draft animation which expects append-only updates
   - Added `updateStatusMessage()` and `deleteStatusMessage()` helper functions
   - Modified `editNowInner()` to call `updateStatusMessage()` in draft mode
   - Added cleanup in finally block to delete status message

2. **sessionManager.ts changes:**
   - Tool progress lines now only show the **first word** (tool kind like "Find", "Read", "ls") + duration instead of full title
   - Renamed `TOOL_LINE_NAME_MAX` to `TOOL_KIND_MAX` (64 → 24)
   - Added logic to update tool titles when `tool_call_update` events arrive with complete titles
   - The title gets written back to both `_toolTitles` map and the timeline entry
   - Triggers `onUpdate` when title changes to refresh the progress display

3. **telegram-ui.ts changes:**
   - `renderReply()` now accepts optional `replyHeader` parameter which gets prepended
   - `renderDraftReply()` completely refactored: now only renders header + body + cursor
   - Removed thinking/tools from draft frames - they're now in the separate status channel
   - New function `renderStatusMessage()` extracted to render just tools + thinking for the status message
   - Draft is now truly append-only (no volatile content that changes mid-text)

The commit message mentioned this "roots out draft animation jitter" - the problem was that Telegram's draft animation assumes append-only updates (it fades in the tail based on length delta). 

By splitting into two channels, tool progress and thinking updates no longer interfere with the draft's animation. The status message handles all the volatile content that changes frequently—emoji flips, duration counters, thinking windows—while the draft stays clean and append-only. This eliminates the visual regression where already-displayed text would appear to shift backward when mid-stream updates occurred.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Implemented dual-channel streaming to fix Telegram draft animation jitter</title>
  <subtitle>Split volatile progress indicators into separate status message, keeping draft purely append-only</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Draft frames now contain only header + body + cursor with no mid-text volatile content</fact>
    <fact>Tool progress and thinking preview moved to independent status message sent with disable_notification and edited in-place</fact>
    <fact>Status message created on first update, edited via editMessageText, deleted when turn finalizes</fact>
    <fact>Tool progress lines simplified to show only first word (tool kind like Read/Find/ls) plus duration instead of full title</fact>
    <fact>tool_call_update events with complete titles now write back to timeline entries and trigger display refresh</fact>
    <fact>renderDraftReply refactored to exclude thinking/tools; new renderStatusMessage function extracted for volatile content</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Implemented a dual-channel streaming architecture to eliminate Telegram draft message animation jitter. Telegram's draft API uses append-only animation semantics: the client displays the first N characters immediately and fades in the tail based on length delta. When volatile content (tool progress emoji flips, second counts, window sliding) changed mid-text, it violated this assumption and caused visible text regression where already-displayed content appeared to move backward. The fix splits streaming into two independent channels: (1) draft message containing only stable append-only content (header + body + cursor), and (2) a separate status message for volatile progress indicators (tool timeline + thinking preview) that gets edited in-place using editMessageText with no fade animation. The status message is sent once with disable_notification to avoid spam, updated on each tick, and deleted in the finally block when the turn completes. Tool progress lines were also simplified to show only the first word (tool kind) plus duration instead of full titles with parameters, reducing visual noise. The tool_call_update handler now writes complete titles back to timeline entries when they arrive, ensuring both progress display and logs get full tool names.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/run-prompt.ts</file>
    <file>src/sessionManager.ts</file>
    <file>src/telegram-ui.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/run-prompt.ts</file>
    <file>src/sessionManager.ts</file>
    <file>src/telegram-ui.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

  <outcome>"{\"type\":\"text\",\"file\":{\"filePath\":\"G:\\\\AI\\\\telegram-kiro-bridge-main\\\\src\\\\reply-dedup.ts\",\"content\":\"/**\\n * Reply Dedup — 防止 specialist/parallel delegate 結果重複注入主 session。\\n *\\n * 機制：對每個 (chatId, summaryHash) 做 5 分鐘時間窗去重。\\n * 如果同一個 chatId 在 5 分鐘內收到相同 summary hash 的結果，\\n * 視為重複並跳過注入。\\n *\\n * 觸發場景：\\n * - retry 邏輯導致 specialist 重跑同一任務\\n * - goal continuation 過程中重複觸發 delegate\\n * - 網路/timeout retry 導致結果雙重回傳\\n */\\nimport { createHash } from \\\"node:crypto\\\";\\n\\nconst DEDUP_WINDOW_MS = 5 * 60 * 1000; // 5 分鐘\\n\\ninterface DedupEntry {\\n  hash: string;\\n  timestamp: number;\\n}\\n\\n// chatId → 最近的 dedup entries\\nconst _cache = new Map<number, DedupEntry[]>();\\n\\n/**\\n * 計算文字的去重 hash（取前 2000 字做 SHA-256 前 16 hex）。\\n * 用前 2000 字是因為 specialist summary 最長就 2000。\\n */\\nfunction computeHash(text: string): string {\\n  const input = text.slice(0, 2000);\\n  return createHash(\\\"sha256\\\").update(input, \\\"utf8\\\").digest(\\\"hex\\\").slice(0, 16);\\n}\\n\\n/**\\n * 檢查是否重複。若不重複則登記並回傳 false；若重複回傳 true。\\n *\\n * @param chatId — Telegram chat ID\\n * @param summary — specialist result summary 文字\\n * @returns true = 重複（應跳過注入）, false = 新的（可以注入）\\n */\\nexport function isDuplicateResult(chatId: number, summary: string): boolean {\\n  const now = Date.now();\\n  const hash = computeHash(summary);\\n\\n  // 取出或初始化 cache\\n  let entries = _cache.get(chatId);\\n  if (!entries) {\\n    entries = [];\\n    _cache.set(chatId, entries);\\n  }\\n\\n  // 清理過期 entries\\n  const cutoff = now - DEDUP_WINDOW_MS;\\n  const fresh = entries.filter((e) => e.timestamp > cutoff);\\n\\n  // 檢查是否有相同 hash\\n  const dup = fresh.some((e) => e.hash === hash);\\n  if (dup) {\\n    _cache.set(chatId, fresh);\\n    return true; // 重複\\n  }\\n\\n  // 登記新 entry\\n  fresh.push({ hash, timestamp: now });\\n  _cache.set(chatId, fresh);\\n  return false; // 不重複\\n}\\n\\n/**\\n * 清除指定 chatId 的 dedup cache（例如 /reset 時）。\\n */\\nexport function clearDedupCache(chatId?: number): void {\\n  if (chatId !== undefined) {\\n    _cache.delete(chatId);\\n  } else {\\n    _cache.clear();\\n  }\\n}\\n\",\"numLines\":79,\"startLine\":1,\"totalLines\":79}}"</outcome>

The user is asking me to observe a session where they're checking if the bridge needs any optimization. Let me look at what's been happening:

1. First, they read a handoff document about a 2026-07-11 code review where 25 findings were addressed
2. They checked recent commits and file sizes
3. They looked at the diff since cdf1ff3 showing recent fixes
4. They grepped for synchronous file I/O and found 200+ instances
5. They grepped for timers/intervals and found several polling patterns
6. They checked data structure allocations (Maps, Sets, Arrays)
7. They looked at the recent diff for run-prompt.ts, telegram-ui.ts, sessionManager.ts showing the dual-channel streaming implementation
8. They read portions of run-prompt.ts focusing on streaming logic
9. They read reply-dedup.ts

This appears to be an investigation/discovery session rather than actual optimization work being performed. The user is examining the codebase to identify potential optimization opportunities. They've discovered:

- Extensive synchronous file I/O (200+ instances)
- Polling timers (relay polling every 3s, session sweeper every 60s)
- Recent streaming architecture improvements (dual-channel)
- Data structure patterns

But no actual optimization changes have been made yet beyond what was already in the commits (the streaming fix, memory leak fixes, etc.).

Since this is a discovery/analysis session, I should create an observation that captures what was learned about potential optimization areas.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Bridge optimization audit identified file I/O and polling patterns as improvement candidates</title>
  <subtitle>200+ synchronous file operations and multiple interval timers found; streaming architecture already optimized</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Synchronous file I/O appears 200+ times across src/ directory with high-traffic modules affected: run-prompt.ts, index.ts, sessionManager.ts</fact>
    <fact>Memory-intensive modules use blocking reads extensively: facts-store.ts (25+ calls), memory.ts, page-index.ts, ingest-ripple.ts</fact>
    <fact>Four active setInterval timers: relay polling (3000ms), session sweeper (60000ms), heartbeat timer, podcast timer</fact>
    <fact>Recent commits already addressed streaming performance with dual-channel architecture and fixed atomicJson memory leak</fact>
    <fact>reply-dedup.ts implements 5-minute hash-based deduplication window using in-memory Map cache</fact>
    <fact>Data structure allocations show extensive Map/Set usage for runtime state tracking</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Conducted optimization audit of telegram-kiro-bridge codebase to identify performance improvement opportunities. Analysis revealed two primary optimization candidates: (1) blocking file I/O with 200+ instances of readFileSync, writeFileSync, existsSync across 40+ source files, heavily concentrated in memory management modules (facts-store.ts, page-index.ts, ingest-ripple.ts) and core logic (run-prompt.ts at 1837 lines, sessionManager.ts at 1190 lines), and (2) polling-based timers with relay checking for messages every 3 seconds and session cleanup running every minute. Recent work already addressed streaming performance issues—the dual-channel architecture (commit 136f0c0) eliminated Telegram draft animation jitter by splitting volatile progress indicators into a separate status message, and the atomicJson memory leak (commit 8d0b8fa) was fixed with compare-and-delete cleanup. The codebase shows mature patterns like reply deduplication using SHA-256 hashes with 5-minute time windows and coalesced async runners for concurrency control. Potential next optimizations: convert high-frequency sync file ops to async, replace relay polling with webhook-based event notification, and evaluate cache strategies for frequently-read config files.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/run-prompt.ts</file>
    <file>src/sessionManager.ts</file>
    <file>src/telegram-ui.ts</file>
    <file>src/facts-store.ts</file>
    <file>src/memory.ts</file>
    <file>src/page-index.ts</file>
    <file>src/ingest-ripple.ts</file>
    <file>src/relay.ts</file>
    <file>src/reply-dedup.ts</file>
    <file>src/atomicJson.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
The user is observing a session where someone is checking the bridge codebase for optimization opportunities. Let me see what's being discovered:

1. They've looked at recent commits showing fixes for memory leaks and streaming improvements
2. They grepped for synchronous file I/O operations and found 200+ instances
3. They checked for setInterval timers and found polling patterns
4. They looked at the askRegistry implementation which has a MAX_ENTRIES limit of 500 with FIFO eviction
5. They're now looking at sessionManager's sweeper and toolTimeline/history management

This appears to be an audit phase where they're identifying potential optimization targets. The key findings so far:
- Extensive sync file I/O (potential event loop blocking)
- Polling timers (relay every 3s, session sweeper every 60s)
- Memory management patterns (Map-based caches with bounded sizes)
- Recent fixes already addressed some issues (atomicJson memory leak, dual-channel streaming)

The askRegistry shows a bounded cache pattern (500 max, FIFO eviction when full), and the sessionManager has a sweeper interval for idle session cleanup.

I should record this as a discovery observation about the bounded cache patterns and potential memory management strategies already in place.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Bridge implements bounded cache eviction patterns to prevent unbounded memory growth</title>
  <subtitle>AskRegistry and SessionManager use FIFO eviction with hard caps to limit memory footprint</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>AskRegistry maintains max 500 entries using FIFO eviction when full: oldest message ID deleted before inserting new entry</fact>
    <fact>SessionManager runs sweeper every 60_000ms to clean idle sessions, with sweeper.unref() to allow process shutdown</fact>
    <fact>toolTimeline keeps last 5 tool events for display via slice(-5) but full timeline preserved for logging</fact>
    <fact>Session state resets on each turn: buffer, toolLog, toolTimeline, thinkingBuffer all cleared by resetTurn()</fact>
    <fact>reply-dedup.ts uses 5-minute time window with automatic pruning of expired entries on each check</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Analysis of memory management patterns revealed the bridge implements bounded cache strategies to prevent unbounded growth. The AskRegistry (telegram-ui.ts) enforces a hard cap of 500 entries (ASK_MAX_ENTRIES), using FIFO eviction: when the map reaches capacity, the oldest message ID is deleted before inserting a new entry. This prevents the ask button callback registry from growing indefinitely in long-running sessions. The SessionManager uses a sweeper interval running every 60 seconds to garbage-collect idle sessions, with unref() called to allow the process to exit cleanly without waiting for the timer. Tool timeline displays only the last 5 events (toolTimeline.slice(-5)) for UI rendering while preserving the full timeline for diagnostics. Per-turn state (buffer, toolLog, toolTimeline, thinkingBuffer) is reset at the start of each prompt turn to prevent accumulation. The reply deduplication system automatically prunes expired entries older than 5 minutes on each check. These patterns show deliberate memory discipline with hard caps and periodic cleanup to prevent long-running bridge instances from leaking memory.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/telegram-ui.ts</file>
    <file>src/sessionManager.ts</file>
    <file>src/reply-dedup.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
The user is conducting an optimization audit of the telegram-kiro-bridge codebase. I've been observing the session and documenting findings. Let me review what's been discovered:

1. **Synchronous file I/O**: 200+ instances of blocking file operations across 40+ files
2. **Interval timers**: 4 setInterval timers (relay polling 3s, session sweeper 60s, heartbeat, podcast)
3. **Recent fixes**: 
   - Dual-channel streaming architecture fixed draft animation jitter
   - atomicJson memory leak fixed with compare-and-delete cleanup
   - MCP memory crash fixed by removing config.js dependency
   - CONTINUE token added to observerTransformer whitelist
4. **Memory management**: Bounded caches with FIFO eviction (AskRegistry 500 cap, reply-dedup 5min window)
5. **Dependencies**: Uses grammy, better-sqlite3, @xenova/transformers, ws

The most recent commands are checking:
- resolveMemoryDir function location
- ingest-ripple imports (to understand the MCP memory fix)
- CONTINUE token handling

This looks like the user is wrapping up the audit and verifying recent fixes. Let me document these final discoveries.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Bridge code review completed with no critical optimization gaps found</title>
  <subtitle>Recent commits addressed memory leaks and streaming issues; remaining work is incremental file I/O async conversion</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>resolveMemoryDir() function exists in facts-store.ts line 39 as standalone helper used by ingest-ripple.ts to avoid config.js dependency</fact>
    <fact>CONTINUE token properly handled in observerTransformer.ts with regex at line 65 and whitelist entry at line 717</fact>
    <fact>mcp-memory.ts imports onFactIngested from ingest-ripple.js, completing the dependency loop fix</fact>
    <fact>Project uses TypeScript 5.6.2 with grammy for Telegram, better-sqlite3 for storage, @xenova/transformers for embeddings</fact>
    <fact>Code follows structured token protocol with bounded limits: BALANCED_REASON_MAX=500, SCHEDULE_MAX_PER_MSG=3, SKILL_USED_MAX_PER_MSG=5</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Completed comprehensive optimization audit of telegram-kiro-bridge codebase. Review confirmed recent commits (cdf1ff3..136f0c0) successfully addressed the most critical performance issues: dual-channel streaming architecture eliminated Telegram draft jitter by separating volatile progress indicators from append-only draft content, atomicJson memory leak was fixed with compare-and-delete cleanup in writePendingByPath map, and MCP memory subprocess crash was resolved by extracting resolveMemoryDir() helper to break the config.js circular dependency. The codebase demonstrates mature engineering patterns including bounded caches with FIFO eviction (AskRegistry caps at 500 entries), time-windowed deduplication (5-minute SHA-256 hash windows), and structured token protocols with hard limits to prevent unbounded data. Remaining optimization opportunities are incremental: converting high-frequency synchronous file I/O operations to async (200+ instances concentrated in facts-store.ts, memory.ts, page-index.ts) and potentially replacing relay's 3-second polling with webhook-based event notification. No critical gaps requiring immediate attention were found; the bridge is production-ready with solid memory discipline and defensive programming patterns throughout.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/facts-store.ts</file>
    <file>src/mcp-memory.ts</file>
    <file>src/observerTransformer.ts</file>
    <file>package.json</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Bridge dependency structure verified as clean after ingest-ripple refactor</title>
  <subtitle>facts-store.ts remains dependency-free for MCP server use; circular config.js dependency eliminated</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>facts-store.ts imports only Node.js built-ins (node:fs, node:crypto, node:path) plus topic-rules.js for fact classification</fact>
    <fact>mcp-memory.ts successfully imports facts-store.js, page-index.js, and ingest-ripple.js without triggering config.js load</fact>
    <fact>resolveMemoryDir() function duplicates MEMORY_DIR resolution logic from config.ts without importing it, defaulting to F:\AI\AIMemory</fact>
    <fact>Bridge uses heavyweight dependencies strategically: @xenova/transformers for embedding-based routing, better-sqlite3 for memory-db and routing-log, electron for desktop-pet WebSocket UI</fact>
    <fact>facts-store.ts design explicitly keeps it dependency-free as documented in line 16 comment: "This module is imported by the stdio MCP server so it stays dep-free"</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Final dependency structure verification confirmed the ingest-ripple refactor successfully eliminated the circular dependency that caused MCP memory subprocess crashes. The facts-store.ts module maintains its design goal of staying dependency-free—importing only Node.js built-ins and topic-rules.js—so it can be safely loaded by the stdio MCP server subprocess without triggering config.js validation that requires TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN. The mcp-memory.ts server now successfully imports facts-store.js, page-index.js, and ingest-ripple.js in the subprocess environment. The resolveMemoryDir() helper function duplicates the MEMORY_DIR environment variable resolution logic (defaulting to F:\AI\AIMemory) to avoid importing config.ts entirely. The bridge's heavyweight dependencies are used strategically: @xenova/transformers powers embedding-based NotebookLM routing with local ONNX models, better-sqlite3 provides FTS5 full-text search for memory-db and routing-log persistence, and electron enables the desktop-pet WebSocket-based animated UI. The dependency graph is now properly layered with no circular imports.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/facts-store.ts</file>
    <file>src/mcp-memory.ts</file>
    <file>src/embed-router.ts</file>
    <file>package.json</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

If `scope` is provided, results are filtered to\\n * only the specified domain (for specialist use).\\n */\\nexport async function runEnrichment(\\n  userMsg: string,\\n  userId: number | string,\\n  recentFacts?: Set<string>,\\n  scope?: EnrichmentScope,\\n): Promise<string> {\\n  let prefix = \\\"\\\";\\n\\n  // 1. NotebookLM routing\\n  if (isEmbedRouterReady()) {\\n    try {\\n      const titleFilter = scope?.notebooks?.length\\n        ? new Set(scope.notebooks)\\n        : undefined;\\n      const hits = await routeByEmbedding(userMsg, titleFilter);\\n      if (hits.length > 0) {\\n        const hitLines = hits.map(\\n          (h) => `- 《${h.title}》(id=${h.notebookId}, score=${h.score.toFixed(3)}, strategy=${h.strategy})`,\\n        );\\n        prefix +=\\n          `[NotebookLM routing — embedding match]\\\\n` +\\n          `以下 notebook 與使用者問題語意相近，請用 notebook_query 查詢後以該 strategy 格式回答：\\\\n` +\\n          hitLines.join(\\\"\\\\n\\\") +\\n          `\\\\n[End routing]\\\\n\\\\n`;\\n      }\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.warn(\\\"[enrichment] notebooklm routing failed:\\\", err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // 2. Wiki retrieval\\n  const wikiHitSlugs = new Set<string>();\\n  if (isWikiRetrievalReady()) {\\n    try {\\n      const slugFilter = scope?.wikiPages?.length\\n        ? new Set(scope.wikiPages)\\n        : undefined;\\n      const hits = await retrieveWikiPages(userMsg, slugFilter);\\n      // Dedup: skip wiki pages already injected in a prior turn\\n      const fresh = recentFacts\\n        ? hits.filter((h) => !recentFacts.has(`wiki:${h.slug}`))\\n        : hits;\\n      if (fresh.length > 0) {\\n        for (const h of fresh) {\\n          wikiHitSlugs.add(h.slug);\\n          recentFacts?.add(`wiki:${h.slug}`);\\n          logHit(\\\"wiki\\\", h.slug, \\\"wiki-retrieval\\\");\\n        }\\n        prefix = formatWikiBlock(fresh) + prefix;\\n      }\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.warn(\\\"[enrichment] wiki retrieval failed:\\\", err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // 3. Memory recall\\n  if (isMemoryRecallReady()) {\\n    try {\\n      const includeTopics = scope?.topics?.length\\n        ? new Set(scope.topics)\\n        : undefined;\\n      const hits = await recallRelevantFacts(\\n        userMsg, userId, recentFacts, wikiHitSlugs, includeTopics,\\n      );\\n      if (hits.length > 0) {\\n        // Track injected facts for cross-turn dedup\\n        if (recentFacts) {\\n          for (const h of hits) for (const f of h.facts) recentFacts.add(f);\\n        }\\n        for (const h of hits) logHit(\\\"fact\\\", h.topic, \\\"memory-recall\\\");\\n        prefix = formatRecallBlock(hits) + prefix;\\n      }\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.warn(\\\"[enrichment] memory recall failed:\\\", err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // 4. Index retrieval (PageIndex tree-based document chunks)\\n  if (isIndexRetrievalReady() && !scope) {\\n    try {\\n      const hits = await retrieveIndexChunks(userMsg);\\n      const fresh = recentFacts\\n        ? hits.filter((h) => !recentFacts.has(`idx:${h.slug}`))\\n        : hits;\\n      if (fresh.length > 0) {\\n        for (const h of fresh) {\\n          recentFacts?.add(`idx:${h.slug}`);\\n          logHit(\\\"index\\\", h.slug, \\\"index-retrieval\\\");\\n        }\\n        prefix += formatIndexBlock(fresh);\\n      }\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.warn(\\\"[enrichment] index retrieval failed:\\\", err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  return applyPrefixCap(prefix);\\n}\\n\\n// ─── Enrichment Prefix Cap ─────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\n/**\\n * Section markers used to identify removable blocks in priority order (lowest first).\\n * When the enrichment prefix exceeds the character cap, sections are stripped\\n * starting from the lowest-priority one until it fits.\\n */\\nconst SECTION_PRIORITY: Array<{ start: string; end: string; label: string }> = [\\n  { start: \\\"[Document indexes\\\", end: \\\"[End indexes]\\\", label: \\\"index-retrieval\\\" },\\n  { start: \\\"[Memory recall\\\", end: \\\"[End memory recall]\\\", label: \\\"memory-recall\\\" },\\n  { start: \\\"[Wiki retrieval\\\", end: \\\"[End wiki retrieval]\\\", label: \\\"wiki-retrieval\\\" },\\n  // NotebookLM routing and Skill routing are kept (short + high-value routing directives)\\n];\\n\\n/**\\n * If the prefix exceeds config.enrichmentPrefixMaxChars, strip lowest-priority\\n * sections until it fits. Returns the (possibly trimmed) prefix.\\n */\\nfunction applyPrefixCap(prefix: string): string {\\n  const cap = config.enrichmentPrefixMaxChars;\\n  if (prefix.length <= cap) return prefix;\\n\\n  let result = prefix;\\n  for (const section of SECTION_PRIORITY) {\\n    if (result.length <= cap) break;\\n\\n    const startIdx = result.indexOf(section.start);\\n    if (startIdx < 0) continue;\\n    const endIdx = result.indexOf(section.end, startIdx);\\n    if (endIdx < 0) continue;\\n\\n    const removed = result.slice(startIdx, endIdx + section.end.length);\\n    result = result.slice(0, startIdx) + result.slice(endIdx + section.end.length);\\n    // Clean up leading/trailing newlines from removal\\n    result = result.replace(/\\\\n{3,}/g, \\\"\\\\n\\\\n\\\");\\n\\n    console.log(\\n      `[enrichment] prefix cap: stripped [${section.label}] (${removed.length} chars), ` +\\n      `remaining ${result.length}/${cap}`,\\n    );\\n  }\\n\\n  // If still over cap after stripping all removable sections, hard-truncate\\n  if (result.length > cap) {\\n    console.warn(\\n      `[enrichment] prefix still over cap after stripping (${result.length}/${cap}), hard-truncating`,\\n    );\\n    result = result.slice(0, cap) + \\\"\\\\n[...enrichment truncated]\\\\n\\\";\\n  }\\n\\n  return result;\\n}\\n\",\"numLines\":184,\"startLine\":1,\"totalLines\":184}}"</outcome>

The user is monitoring a primary session where they're checking the bridge codebase for optimization opportunities. Let me observe what's happening:

1. They've read various parts of index.ts showing the main message handler with the enrichment pipeline
2. They checked transitive imports to verify no config.js circular dependencies
3. They checked token estimation uses gpt-tokenizer
4. They verified local-llm and embed-router don't have local imports (use only built-ins and external deps)

The main observation here is they're looking at the enrichment pipeline in the main message handler - this is a significant performance-critical path that runs on every user message. Let me document what I see:

The enrichment pipeline runs multiple async operations sequentially:
1. Specialist pre-filter (prefilterSpecialistRoute + shouldRoute)
2. Reply context extraction
3. NotebookLM embedding routing
4. Wiki retrieval
5. Memory recall
6. Skill routing  
7. Intent classification

Each of these can add latency. The code shows timing logging for the total enrichment if it exceeds 500ms.

This is important context about the architecture - the bridge has a sophisticated multi-stage enrichment pipeline that augments every user message with relevant context before sending to the LLM.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Bridge implements sequential enrichment pipeline with 7-stage context injection</title>
  <subtitle>Every user message passes through specialist routing, wiki retrieval, memory recall, skill routing, and intent classification before LLM</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Main message handler (index.ts line 1050+) runs 7-stage enrichment pipeline sequentially: specialist pre-filter, reply context, NotebookLM routing, wiki retrieval, memory recall, skill routing, intent classification</fact>
    <fact>Enrichment pipeline skipped entirely when activeProvider is "local" to avoid overhead on local LLM calls</fact>
    <fact>Per-session deduplication prevents re-injecting same wiki pages (session.injectedWikiSlugs) and skills (session.injectedSkills) across turns</fact>
    <fact>Pipeline logs total time if enrichment exceeds 500ms threshold indicating performance monitoring</fact>
    <fact>Specialist pre-filter uses dual approach: prefilterSpecialistRoute (local LLM) + shouldRoute (routing coordinator) for semantic classification</fact>
    <fact>Intent classifier has LLM fallback (classifyIntentLLM) for 25-80 char messages when embedding classifier skips long text</fact>
    <fact>Each enrichment stage wraps await in try-catch to fail gracefully without breaking the pipeline</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Discovered the bridge's main message handler implements a sophisticated 7-stage sequential enrichment pipeline that augments every user message with contextual information before sending to the LLM. The pipeline runs: (1) specialist pre-filter using local LLM to suggest routing to domain-specific agents, (2) Telegram reply context extraction to understand quoted messages, (3) NotebookLM embedding-based routing for semantic matches, (4) wiki page retrieval injecting relevant documentation, (5) memory recall fetching semantically similar facts from user's topic shards, (6) skill routing suggesting relevant commands, and (7) intent classification to detect if user meant to invoke a slash command. The pipeline is performance-aware: entirely skipped when activeProvider is "local" to avoid overhead, logs timing when total enrichment exceeds 500ms, and implements per-session deduplication to prevent re-injecting the same wiki pages or skills across multiple turns. Each stage fails gracefully with try-catch wrappers so one failure doesn't break the chain. The specialist pre-filter uses a dual approach combining local LLM semantic routing with a routing coordinator for higher accuracy. Intent classification has an LLM fallback for mid-length messages where the embedding classifier's length limits apply. This architecture explains why the bridge needs bounded caches and memory discipline—the enrichment pipeline runs on every message and accumulates state across turns.</narrative>

    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>trade-off</concept>

  <outcome>"{\"type\":\"text\",\"file\":{\"filePath\":\"G:\\\\AI\\\\telegram-kiro-bridge-main\\\\src\\\\enrichment.ts\",\"content\":\"/**\\n * enrichment.ts — Shared dynamic enrichment logic (NotebookLM routing,\\n * wiki retrieval, memory recall) used by both the main agent turn and\\n * specialist proxy/delegation.\\n */\\n\\nimport { isEmbedRouterReady, routeByEmbedding } from \\\"./embed-router.js\\\";\\nimport { isWikiRetrievalReady, retrieveWikiPages, formatWikiBlock } from \\\"./wiki-retrieval.js\\\";\\nimport { isMemoryRecallReady, recallRelevantFacts, formatRecallBlock } from \\\"./memory-recall.js\\\";\\nimport { isIndexRetrievalReady, retrieveIndexChunks, formatIndexBlock } from \\\"./index-retrieval.js\\\";\\nimport { logHit } from \\\"./hit-log.js\\\";\\nimport { config } from \\\"./config.js\\\";\\n\\n// ─── Types ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\n/** Scope filter — limits enrichment to a specialist's domain. */\\nexport interface EnrichmentScope {\\n  /** Only match these wiki page slugs. */\\n  wikiPages?: string[];\\n  /** Only match notebooks whose title is in this list. */\\n  notebooks?: string[];\\n  /** Only recall from these topic names. */\\n  topics?: string[];\\n}\\n\\n// ─── Public API ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\n/**\\n * Run the three enrichment stages and return a prefix string to prepend\\n * to the user message. If `scope` is provided, results are filtered to\\n * only the specified domain (for specialist use).\\n */\\nexport async function runEnrichment(\\n  userMsg: string,\\n  userId: number | string,\\n  recentFacts?: Set<string>,\\n  scope?: EnrichmentScope,\\n): Promise<string> {\\n  let prefix = \\\"\\\";\\n\\n  // 1. NotebookLM routing\\n  if (isEmbedRouterReady()) {\\n    try {\\n      const titleFilter = scope?.notebooks?.length\\n        ? new Set(scope.notebooks)\\n        : undefined;\\n      const hits = await routeByEmbedding(userMsg, titleFilter);\\n      if (hits.length > 0) {\\n        const hitLines = hits.map(\\n          (h) => `- 《${h.title}》(id=${h.notebookId}, score=${h.score.toFixed(3)}, strategy=${h.strategy})`,\\n        );\\n        prefix +=\\n          `[NotebookLM routing — embedding match]\\\\n` +\\n          `以下 notebook 與使用者問題語意相近，請用 notebook_query 查詢後以該 strategy 格式回答：\\\\n` +\\n          hitLines.join(\\\"\\\\n\\\") +\\n          `\\\\n[End routing]\\\\n\\\\n`;\\n      }\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.warn(\\\"[enrichment] notebooklm routing failed:\\\", err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // 2. Wiki retrieval\\n  const wikiHitSlugs = new Set<string>();\\n  if (isWikiRetrievalReady()) {\\n    try {\\n      const slugFilter = scope?.wikiPages?.length\\n        ? new Set(scope.wikiPages)\\n        : undefined;\\n      const hits = await retrieveWikiPages(userMsg, slugFilter);\\n      // Dedup: skip wiki pages already injected in a prior turn\\n      const fresh = recentFacts\\n        ? hits.filter((h) => !recentFacts.has(`wiki:${h.slug}`))\\n        : hits;\\n      if (fresh.length > 0) {\\n        for (const h of fresh) {\\n          wikiHitSlugs.add(h.slug);\\n          recentFacts?.add(`wiki:${h.slug}`);\\n          logHit(\\\"wiki\\\", h.slug, \\\"wiki-retrieval\\\");\\n        }\\n        prefix = formatWikiBlock(fresh) + prefix;\\n      }\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.warn(\\\"[enrichment] wiki retrieval failed:\\\", err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // 3. Memory recall\\n  if (isMemoryRecallReady()) {\\n    try {\\n      const includeTopics = scope?.topics?.length\\n        ? new Set(scope.topics)\\n        : undefined;\\n      const hits = await recallRelevantFacts(\\n        userMsg, userId, recentFacts, wikiHitSlugs, includeTopics,\\n      );\\n      if (hits.length > 0) {\\n        // Track injected facts for cross-turn dedup\\n        if (recentFacts) {\\n          for (const h of hits) for (const f of h.facts) recentFacts.add(f);\\n        }\\n        for (const h of hits) logHit(\\\"fact\\\", h.topic, \\\"memory-recall\\\");\\n        prefix = formatRecallBlock(hits) + prefix;\\n      }\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.warn(\\\"[enrichment] memory recall failed:\\\", err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // 4. Index retrieval (PageIndex tree-based document chunks)\\n  if (isIndexRetrievalReady() && !scope) {\\n    try {\\n      const hits = await retrieveIndexChunks(userMsg);\\n      const fresh = recentFacts\\n        ? hits.filter((h) => !recentFacts.has(`idx:${h.slug}`))\\n        : hits;\\n      if (fresh.length > 0) {\\n        for (const h of fresh) {\\n          recentFacts?.add(`idx:${h.slug}`);\\n          logHit(\\\"index\\\", h.slug, \\\"index-retrieval\\\");\\n        }\\n        prefix += formatIndexBlock(fresh);\\n      }\\n    } catch (err) {\\n      console.warn(\\\"[enrichment] index retrieval failed:\\\", err);\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  return applyPrefixCap(prefix);\\n}\\n\\n// ─── Enrichment Prefix Cap ─────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\n/**\\n * Section markers used to identify removable blocks in priority order (lowest first).\\n * When the enrichment prefix exceeds the character cap, sections are stripped\\n * starting from the lowest-priority one until it fits.\\n */\\nconst SECTION_PRIORITY: Array<{ start: string; end: string; label: string }> = [\\n  { start: \\\"[Document indexes\\\", end: \\\"[End indexes]\\\", label: \\\"index-retrieval\\\" },\\n  { start: \\\"[Memory recall\\\", end: \\\"[End memory recall]\\\", label: \\\"memory-recall\\\" },\\n  { start: \\\"[Wiki retrieval\\\", end: \\\"[End wiki retrieval]\\\", label: \\\"wiki-retrieval\\\" },\\n  // NotebookLM routing and Skill routing are kept (short + high-value routing directives)\\n];\\n\\n/**\\n * If the prefix exceeds config.enrichmentPrefixMaxChars, strip lowest-priority\\n * sections until it fits. Returns the (possibly trimmed) prefix.\\n */\\nfunction applyPrefixCap(prefix: string): string {\\n  const cap = config.enrichmentPrefixMaxChars;\\n  if (prefix.length <= cap) return prefix;\\n\\n  let result = prefix;\\n  for (const section of SECTION_PRIORITY) {\\n    if (result.length <= cap) break;\\n\\n    const startIdx = result.indexOf(section.start);\\n    if (startIdx < 0) continue;\\n    const endIdx = result.indexOf(section.end, startIdx);\\n    if (endIdx < 0) continue;\\n\\n    const removed = result.slice(startIdx, endIdx + section.end.length);\\n    result = result.slice(0, startIdx) + result.slice(endIdx + section.end.length);\\n    // Clean up leading/trailing newlines from removal\\n    result = result.replace(/\\\\n{3,}/g, \\\"\\\\n\\\\n\\\");\\n\\n    console.log(\\n      `[enrichment] prefix cap: stripped [${section.label}] (${removed.length} chars), ` +\\n      `remaining ${result.length}/${cap}`,\\n    );\\n  }\\n\\n  // If still over cap after stripping all removable sections, hard-truncate\\n  if (result.length > cap) {\\n    console.warn(\\n      `[enrichment] prefix still over cap after stripping (${result.length}/${cap}), hard-truncating`,\\n    );\\n    result = result.slice(0, cap) + \\\"\\\\n[...enrichment truncated]\\\\n\\\";\\n  }\\n\\n  return result;\\n}\\n\",\"numLines\":184,\"startLine\":1,\"totalLines\":184}}"</outcome>

Returns null if model not ready.\\n * Reusable by other modules that need embedding vectors.\\n */\\nexport async function embedText(text: string): Promise<Float32Array | null> {\\n  if (!embedder) return null;\\n  return embed(text);\\n}\\n\\nexport function cosineSim(a: Float32Array, b: Float32Array): number {\\n  let dot = 0;\\n  for (let i = 0; i < a.length; i++) dot += a[i] * b[i];\\n  return dot; // already L2-normalized → dot = cosine\\n}\\n\\n// ─── Internals ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\\n\\nasync function embed(text: string): Promise<Float32Array> {\\n  // Strip chat-template special tokens that some models leak into output.\\n  // The BGE tokenizer rejects them, causing \\\"Disallowed special token\\\" errors.\\n  const clean = text.replace(/<\\\\|[a-z_]+\\\\|>/g, \\\"\\\");\\n  const output = await embedder!(clean, { pooling: \\\"mean\\\", normalize: true });\\n  return output.data as Float32Array;\\n}\\n\",\"numLines\":27,\"startLine\":140,\"totalLines\":166}}"</outcome>

content.thinking:
This is very interesting - there's a dedicated enrichment.ts module that encapsulates the enrichment pipeline logic, and it has a configurable prefix cap with priority-based section stripping. This is a sophisticated approach to managing context window usage.

Let me summarize what I've learned:
1. enrichment.ts runs 4 stages: NotebookLM routing, wiki retrieval, memory recall, index retrieval
2. It supports scoping for specialist domains
3. It has a configurable cap (config.enrichmentPrefixMaxChars) with smart priority-based trimming
4. Priority order (lowest to highest): index-retrieval, memory-recall, wiki-retrieval, then NotebookLM/skill routing (kept)
5. Deduplication happens across turns using recentFacts set
6. All embedText calls go through embed-router.ts which uses @xenova/transformers

The review is complete. The bridge shows mature engineering with:
- Bounded memory structures (caps, FIFO eviction, time windows)
- Sequential enrichment pipeline with graceful failure handling
- Recent fixes for memory leaks and streaming issues
- Clean dependency structure after ingest-ripple refactor
- Sophisticated context management with priority-based trimming

The main optimization opportunities are:
1. Async file I/O conversion (200+ sync calls)
2. Replace relay polling with webhooks
3. Potential caching for frequently-read configs

I should create one final observation about the enrichment system.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Bridge enrichment pipeline implements priority-based context budget management</title>
  <subtitle>Four-stage enrichment with configurable cap and intelligent section stripping when context limit exceeded</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>enrichment.ts centralizes four enrichment stages: NotebookLM routing, wiki retrieval, memory recall, index retrieval with optional domain scoping for specialists</fact>
    <fact>Enrichment prefix capped at config.enrichmentPrefixMaxChars with priority-based section stripping: index-retrieval (lowest) → memory-recall → wiki-retrieval → NotebookLM/skill routing (highest, always kept)</fact>
    <fact>Cross-turn deduplication tracks injected content in recentFacts set to prevent re-injecting same wiki pages or facts</fact>
    <fact>All embedding operations route through embed-router.ts using @xenova/transformers pipeline with Xenova/bge-small-zh-v1.5 model</fact>
    <fact>embedText() called 33 times across 12 modules for semantic similarity matching in routing, recall, and classification</fact>
    <fact>config.ts defines memoryDir default as F:\AI\AIMemory resolving from MEMORY_DIR environment variable</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Discovered the bridge's enrichment system is centralized in enrichment.ts as a reusable module serving both main agent turns and specialist proxies. The module runs a 4-stage pipeline: (1) NotebookLM embedding-based routing, (2) wiki page retrieval, (3) memory recall from topic shards, (4) index-based document chunk retrieval. Each stage supports optional domain scoping for specialist-specific enrichment (e.g., only certain notebooks, wiki pages, or topics). The system implements sophisticated context budget management: when the combined enrichment prefix exceeds config.enrichmentPrefixMaxChars, it strips sections in priority order starting with lowest-value content (index-retrieval first, then memory-recall, then wiki-retrieval) while always preserving high-value routing directives (NotebookLM and skill routing). Cross-turn deduplication prevents repeatedly injecting the same wiki pages or facts using a recentFacts set that accumulates across the session. All semantic similarity operations funnel through embed-router.ts which loads @xenova/transformers once at startup and caches embeddings where possible. The embedText() function is called 33 times across 12 modules for routing, recall, and classification tasks, making the embedding model a critical dependency. This architecture explains the bridge's need for bounded memory structures—the enrichment pipeline runs on every message and can generate substantial context prefixes that must be capped to fit within LLM token limits.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>trade-off</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/enrichment.ts</file>
    <file>src/config.ts</file>
    <file>src/embed-router.ts</file>
    <file>src/memory-recall.ts</file>
    <file>src/wiki-retrieval.ts</file>
    <file>src/index-retrieval.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

1. Recent commits analyzed (cdf1ff3..136f0c0):
   - Dual-channel streaming architecture for draft messages
   - atomicJson memory leak fix

4. Memory management patterns:

   - reply-dedup: 5-minute time windows

5. Enrichment pipeline:
   - 7-stage sequential pipeline
   - Priority-based context budget management

6. Configuration:

   - enrichmentPrefixMaxChars with priority stripping
   - memoryDir: F:\AI\AIMemory

The session appears to be wrapping up the optimization review. The last few reads are checking:
- Final render logic in run-prompt.ts
- Embedding implementation details
- Stream edit interval configuration

This looks like a thorough audit that found the codebase is already well-optimized with mature patterns. The main remaining opportunities are incremental (async file I/O conversion, webhook-based relay instead of polling).
